Document [1] (Title: Angiosarcoma -- Treatment / Management) Metastatic disease: Metastatic disease is treated with chemotherapy. Cytotoxic agents, targeted therapy, and Immune checkpoint inhibitors are being explored in treating metastatic disease. Cytotoxic chemotherapy- anthracycline-based regimens are usually the first line of treatment in a patient with any STS. However, in patients with AS, paclitaxel is an effective drug with comparable response rates to anthracyclines. Anthracyclines- In patients with STS, the response rate is between 16-36%. In most cases, a combination with another drug, ifosfamide, certainly increases toxicity but may not benefit the outcome. [14] Liposomal doxorubicin has also shown therapeutic responses in patients with AS. [15] Taxanes: The antiangiogenic properties of taxanes make them useful in patients with AS. After numerous retrospective studies reporting benefits in AS, a small phase II prospective study (ANGIOTAX study) showed an ORR of 17%. However, two of the five patients with responses underwent surgery and achieved a complete pathological response. [16] Since then, multiple studies have confirmed the activity of weekly paclitaxel in patients with AS. [17] Paclitaxel is administered as 80mg/m2 on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle. Other cytotoxic drugs like Ifosfamdie, Gemcitabine, Cisplatin are also active in patients with AS; however, they are usually not used as the first line of treatment. Targeted therapy Anti-VEGF molecule- Since AS is a malignancy arising from vascular endothelium, anti-VEGF therapy, particularly bevacizumab, gained a lot of attention for metastatic AS. However, a prospective phase II study failed to show any benefit. [2] Tyrosine Kinase inhibitors (TKI)- Several TKI (Sorafenib, Pazopanib, Axitinib, and regorafenib) have been tested in patients with AS. The drugs mainly target either VEGF or PDGFRA, which are critical to the growth of AS. Unfortunately, none of these drugs showed a beneficial response, despite clinical activity in patients with AS. Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI). Recent data suggest that ICI may be active in patients with AS. [18] This is yet to be tested in a prospective trial.

Document [15] (Title: Carcinomatous Meningitis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) Several future directions may help improve survival in patients with carcinomatous meningitis. This includes early diagnosis by analyzing circulating tumor DNA in CSF (CSF-ctDNA). These are shed from the cancer cells as a result of apoptosis and necrosis. Identification can help avoid subjecting the patient to high-risk surgeries or biopsies. The amount of CSF-ct DNA can provide information about tumor burden, recurrence, and treatment response. The identification of novel mutations in CSF-ctDNA can also provide newer therapeutic targets. There is a need for discovery of non-invasive methods like ultrasonography or CT scan guided drug delivery. Novel biological agents and immunomodulatory drugs may help in achieving better survival and improved quality of life.

Document [30] (Title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern) The main areas described in the literature for the development of bsmAbs include inhibition of multiple cell surface receptors, multiple ligand blockade, receptor cross-linkage, and recruitment of Fc receptor absent T-cells (which would normally not be active with antibody stimulation due to the absence of Fc receptors. The design and implementation of bsmAbs have proven to be challenging despite the conceptually sound nature of the propositions. For example, catumaxomab was one such agent that was initially approved for malignant ascites (2009) but withdrawn from the market in 2017 despite its success due to adverse events. The mechanism of action has been described as trifunctional. There are two antigen-binding regions and an Fc region present on the molecule. The first antigen-binding region targets a trans-membrane glycoprotein named EpCAM, which can be found on malignant cells. The second antigen-binding region binds CD3 antigens on T-cells of the immune system. The Fc region functions to activate and recruit immune cells, including macrophages and natural killer cells, to act against malignant cells. Overall, catumaxomab activates multiple pathways for immune-mediated cell destruction (i.e., ADCC, ADCP, etc.) and cytokine-mediated cytotoxicity. [11] [12] [13] [14]

Document [9] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 427) Pharmacologic Substance is a broad therapeutic class that encompasses a wide variety of chemical agents and biologics, including the synthetic estrogen ethinylestradiol, the barbiturate subclass barbituráty, and ethanol, which also appears as an excipient in coal‑tar 50 MG/ML medicated shampoo. Within this class, numerous antineoplastic and cytotoxic agents are represented, such as bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlorambucil, chlormethin hydrochlorid, cisplatin (cisplatina), cyclophosphamide anhydrous, dakarbazin, doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, procarbazine (hydrochlorid prokarbazinu), ifosfamid, lomustine (lomustin), mechlorethamine, streptozocin, as well as the vinca‑alkaloids vinblastine sulfate, vincristine sulfate and vinorelbine tartrate. Immunomodulatory and biologic therapies in this group include the checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab and the recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor sargramostim. Collectively, these agents constitute the diverse pharmacologic strategies—alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamid, procarbazine), platinum‑based therapy (cisplatin), nitrogen‑mustard derivatives (dakarbazin, lomustine), antimetabolites (mechlorethamine), vinca‑alkaloids (vinblastine sulfate, vincristine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate), anthracyclines (doxorubicin hydrochloride and liposomal form), bleomycin, monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), antihistamine‑related agent (chlormethin hydrochlorid), immune checkpoint inhibition (pembrolizumab), and cytokine therapy (sargramostim). Vinca‑alkaloid derivatives—vinblastine, vinblastine sulfate, vincristine sulfate, vinorelbine, vinorelbine tartarate, vinorelbin, and vindesin—are used together with doxorubicin, chlormethin hydrochloride, bleomycin sulfate, and other cytotoxics to achieve synergistic disease control. Targeted agents such as brentuximab vedotin and Vintafolide are distinguished from conventional cytotoxics but remain part of the same pharmacologic hierarchy. Immunomodulatory therapy is represented by the colony‑stimulating factor sargramostim (GM‑CSF). Additional pharmaceutical categories mentioned include hormonal agents (ethinylestradiol), barbiturates, iron‑folate supplements (ferrous amino chelate), and direct Factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban), all grouped under the overarching procedure “use of pharmaceuticals,” which also encompasses interventions like inhaled nitric oxide. Collectively, these entities illustrate a multimodal treatment paradigm for Hodgkin disease that combines traditional chemotherapy, targeted immunotherapy, and immune‑modulating agents to optimize patient outcomes.

Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1088) The ABVD chemotherapy regimen incorporates vinblastin as a direct active substance. Vinblastin is one of several agents classified within the group “Vinca alkaloids and analogues, antineoplastic,” which also includes vindesin and vinorelbin. Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2565) Pharmacologic Substance is a broad category that encompasses a wide variety of therapeutic agents, including both individual drugs and drug classes. Barbituráty are identified as a subclass of Pharmacologic Substance, representing the group of barbiturate compounds used for their sedative‑hypnotic properties. Specific agents that are recorded as values for a GDC property within the Pharmacologic Substance category include ethinylestradiol, semuloparin, uproleselan, brentuximab vedotin, chlormethin hydrochlorid, doxorubicin hydrochloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, hydrochlorid prokarbazinu, ifosfamid, sargramostim, and vinorelbin tartarát. These substances span diverse pharmacologic classes: ethinylestradiol is a synthetic estrogen used in hormonal therapy; semuloparin is a low‑molecular‑weight heparin anticoagulant; uproleselan is an experimental selectin inhibitor; brentuximab vedotin is an antibody‑drug conjugate targeting CD30‑positive malignancies; chlormethin hydrochlorid is an antihistamine; doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation are anthracycline chemotherapeutics; hydrochlorid prokarbazinu (procarbazine) is an alkylating agent; ifosfamid (ifosfamide) is a nitrogen‑mustard alkylating chemotherapy; sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production; and vinorelbin tartarát (vinorelbine tartrate) is a vinca‑alkaloid chemotherapy. Together, these examples illustrate the extensive range of drugs classified as Pharmacologic Substances, highlighting their varied mechanisms of action and clinical applications across hormonal therapy, anticoagulation, immunomodulation, and oncology.

Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 83) The AT protocol incorporates the anthracycline doxorubicin (both hydrochloride and liposomal formulations) as a direct component of the R‑CHOP regimen, which is employed in Hodgkin disease; similarly, the COP‑1, COPADEM and Price‑Hill protocols include the Vinca‑alkaloid vinkristin, also listed in R‑CHOP. Vintafolide is a targeted‑therapy agent belonging to the Vinca‑alkaloid family that comprises vinblastine (including vinblastine sulfate), vincristine, vinorelbine (including vinorelbine tartrate), vindesin and related analogues. Cytotoxic treatment of Hodgkin lymphoma utilizes alkylating agents (chlorambucil, dacarbazine, mechlorethamine, procarbazine, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide), the platinum compound cisplatin, antitumor antibiotics (bleomycin sulfate, chlormethine hydrochloride), anthracyclines (doxorubicin hydrochloride, liposomal doxorubicin), as well as lomustine, hydrochloro‑procarbazine, streptozocin and the Vinca‑alkaloids vinblastine, vincristine and vinorelbine, which act on rapidly proliferating malignant lymphocytes to achieve disease control and remission. Targeted and immunotherapeutic options include brentuximab vedotin (a CD30‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate) and pembrolizumab (a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor), while sargramostim (recombinant GM‑CSF) serves as an adjunctive therapy. The pharmacologic repertoire also contains agents such as ethinylestradiol and barbiturates, illustrating the diversity of therapeutic classes in oncology, and inhaled nitric oxide is classified as a child procedure within pharmaceutical‑related interventions.
